text: The report examines Maria Shagina’s July 27, 2021 opinion piece in The Moscow Times, which critiques the U.S.-Germany Nord Stream 2 agreement.
Contrary to the article’s claim that the deal is contingent on Ukraine’s consent, the joint statement did not make completion conditional on Ukraine’s approval.
The text instead outlined measures to address Ukraine’s concerns, though critics questioned the binding nature and implementation mechanisms of those commitments.
It confirmed sanctions as a primary instrument to deter Russian energy coercion, with both the U.S. and Germany pledging readiness to impose new sanctions.
Germany committed to safeguarding and increasing reverse gas flows to Ukraine, but analyses pointed to non-binding language dependent on market conditions.
The statement explicitly aimed to extend Ukraine’s gas transit agreement beyond 2024 and appointed a German special envoy to facilitate an extension of up to ten years.
The U.S.-Germany Climate and Energy Partnership established a Green Fund for Ukraine with initial German contributions of $175 million, targeting $1 billion in investments for decarbonization.
Observers noted that while these elements align with the joint declaration, vague formulations and lack of enforcement mechanisms remain central trustworthiness concerns.
